name: Skincare products (cosmetics)
summary: Cleansers, serums, moisturizers, sunscreens — FDA-regulated cosmetic category with heavy marketing claim/reality gap.

evidence.claim: FDA regulates cosmetics under the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act; cosmetics CANNOT make 'structure-function' claims (e.g. 'reduces wrinkles', 'rebuilds collagen') without becoming drugs requiring premarket approval.

FDA's Sunscreen Final Rule (21 CFR 352) requires SPF testing per ISO 24444 for water resistance and UVA protection ('broad spectrum'). Many SPF claims have been challenged for failing to meet test methodology.
'Clean beauty' has no FDA-defined meaning. Companies set their own ingredient exclusion lists. Independent EWG Skin Deep database scores cosmetics on hazard data but is not a regulator.

criteria.name: active_ingredients
criteria.notes: Concentration matters as much as presence.

criteria.name: active_concentration_percent
criteria.notes: Retinol effective at 0.3-1%; vitamin C at 10-20%; niacinamide at 5-10%.

criteria.name: broad_spectrum
criteria.notes: FDA term for UVA + UVB protection.

confabulationPatterns.reality: FDA prohibits cosmetics from making structure-function claims (E1). Topical collagen molecules are too large to penetrate the dermis where collagen is produced. Retinoids and peptides have some evidence; 'collagen cream' has minimal direct effect.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the product an FDA-approved drug (prescription tretinoin) or a cosmetic? Cosmetic 'collagen' claims are marketing.

confabulationPatterns.reality: 'Clinical' for cosmetics often means a small in-house study (10-30 participants, no control, no peer review). Compare to actual peer-reviewed dermatology research.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the clinical evidence peer-reviewed and published, or in-house marketing data?

confabulationPatterns.reality: 'Clean' has no FDA definition (E3). 'Natural' essential oils can cause more irritation than synthetic ingredients. Marketing language only.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: What is the specific ingredient list? Is the product fragrance-free, hypoallergenic-tested?

confabulationPatterns.reality: FDA proposed capping SPF labeling at 60+ because incremental UVA/UVB protection above SPF 50 is marginal. SPF 100 doesn't double the protection of SPF 50 (E2).
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the SPF figure framed as proportionally more protective than SPF 30/50? Diminishing returns above 50.

confabulationPatterns.reality: Specific to a survey or paid endorsement. Look for dermatologist-tested vs dermatologist-developed vs paid-endorsement; only the third is marketing.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the endorsement specific (board-certified dermatologist X) or generic survey data?

counterfeitSignals.signal: Brand-name skincare from third-party Amazon seller >40% off retail
counterfeitSignals.action: flag — counterfeit cosmetics with contaminated/expired contents are widespread, FDA has issued multiple recalls

compatibilityQuestions: Skin type: dry, oily, combination, sensitive, acne-prone, mature?
Existing routine — what other actives are you using? Some combinations (retinol + AHA, vitamin C + niacinamide) need timing care.

typicalHiddenCosts.name: Replacement cycles (active ingredients oxidize/degrade in 3-6 months)
Patch testing supplies / dermatologist consult if reaction
